description: Detailed LSM tree storage, compaction, disk I/O, and memory monitoring for ArcherDB. Shows internal storage engine metrics for operators familiar with LSM-based databases.

panels.description:
Overall storage subsystem health based on combined metrics.

Green: All metrics within normal thresholds
Yellow: Write or space amplification elevated, compression degraded
Red: Critical amplification levels or compaction issues

Drill down to specific metrics below for details.
panels.title: Storage Health

panels.description:
Write amplification ratio: total bytes written to disk divided by bytes received from clients.

Green (<3x): Excellent - minimal overhead
Yellow (3-5x): Normal for LSM workloads
Red (>5x): High amplification - consider compaction tuning

Note: Value is scaled by 100 internally (300 = 3.0x).
panels.title: Write Amplification

panels.description:
Space amplification ratio: physical storage size divided by logical data size.

Green (<2x): Excellent storage efficiency
Yellow (2-3x): Normal for LSM with active workloads
Red (>3x): High space overhead - compaction may be behind

Note: Value is scaled by 100 internally (200 = 2.0x).
panels.title: Space Amplification

panels.description:
Compression ratio: compressed size divided by original size.

Lower is better (0.5 = 50% of original = 2x compression).
Typical values: 0.3-0.6 depending on data patterns.

Note: Value is scaled by 1000 internally (500 = 0.5 ratio).
panels.title: Compression Ratio

panels.description:
Compression ratio over time. Lower values indicate better compression (0.5 = 2x compression).

Fluctuations may indicate changing data patterns:
- Rising ratio: Data becoming less compressible (already compressed data, random data)
- Falling ratio: More compressible data arriving

Note: Scaled by 1000 internally, divided here for display.
panels.title: Compression Ratio Over Time

panels.description:
Total bytes saved by compression across all compacted data.

Higher values indicate more effective compression. This metric accumulates over time - use rate() for bytes saved per second.
panels.title: Bytes Saved by Compression

panels.description:
Compression effectiveness shown as the inverse ratio (higher = better compression).

Example: 2.5x means data is compressed to 40% of original size.

Calculated as: bytes_in / bytes_out
panels.title: Compression Effectiveness

panels.description:
Size of data at each LSM level. ArcherDB uses a leveled compaction strategy where data flows from L0 (newest) down to L6 (oldest).

Level 0: Contains recently flushed memtables, highest churn
Levels 1-6: Progressively older and larger, more stable

A healthy LSM tree shows larger sizes at lower levels (L0 smallest, L6 largest). If L0 grows too large, writes may stall.
panels.title: LSM Level Sizes

panels.description:
Number of SSTable files at each LSM level. More tables at a level means more potential for read amplification (may need to check multiple files for a key).

L0 typically has fewer but larger tables from recent flushes. Compaction merges tables to reduce count at each level.
panels.title: Tables per Level

panels.description:
Write amplification ratio over time.

This shows how many times data is rewritten due to LSM compaction.
Lower is better - typical values are 5-20x for write-heavy workloads.

Note: Value is scaled by 100 internally, divided for display.
panels.title: Write Amplification Ratio

panels.description:
Rate of bytes written to storage over time.

This includes all compaction, flush, and direct write operations. Higher rates indicate more I/O activity.
panels.title: Bytes Written Rate

panels.description:
Compaction throttle ratio (0-100%).

100% = Full throughput, no throttling
<100% = Compaction is being slowed to prevent overwhelming disk I/O

Throttling protects read latency but can cause write stalls if prolonged.
panels.title: Compaction Throttle

panels.description:
Time taken to complete compaction operations. Shows p50 (typical), p95 (elevated), and p99 (worst case) latencies.

Compaction merges SSTable files to:
- Reduce read amplification
- Reclaim space from deleted/overwritten data
- Move data to appropriate levels

Long compactions can impact write throughput. Consider tuning compaction settings if p99 regularly exceeds 10s.
panels.title: Compaction Duration

panels.description:
Number of compaction operations completed in the last hour.

Compaction frequency depends on write workload:
- High writes: More frequent compactions
- Read-heavy: Fewer compactions
- Zero compactions for extended time: May indicate idle database or stalled compaction
panels.title: Compactions/hour

panels.description:
Bytes read from and written to disk during compaction operations.

Read vs Write ratio indicates compaction efficiency:
- Similar read/write: Mostly moves (efficient)
- Higher read than write: Many deletions being cleaned up
- Higher write than read: Unusual, may indicate issue

Sudden drops may indicate compaction completing or stalling.
panels.title: Compaction Throughput

panels.description:
Total bytes pending compaction across all levels.

High pending bytes indicates compaction is falling behind write rate. This can lead to write stalls if L0 fills up.
panels.title: Compaction Pending Bytes

panels.description:
Number of duplicate blocks detected and deduplicated.

Higher values indicate more repeated data patterns in the workload (common for trajectory data with repeated locations).
panels.title: Blocks Deduplicated

panels.description:
Total storage bytes saved by block deduplication.

This represents the disk space that would have been used by duplicate blocks.
panels.title: Dedup Bytes Saved

panels.description:
Deduplication hit rate over time.

Shows the percentage of blocks that were duplicates. Higher values indicate more duplicate data in the workload.
panels.title: Deduplication Hit Rate

panels.description:
Disk read operation latency percentiles. Shows how long it takes to read data from disk.

For SSDs, typical expectations:
- p50: <1ms
- p95: <5ms  
- p99: <10ms (threshold line)

Higher latencies may indicate:
- Disk saturation
- Noisy neighbors (shared storage)
- Hardware degradation
panels.title: Disk Read Latency

panels.description:
Disk write operation latency percentiles. Shows how long it takes to write and sync data to disk.

For SSDs, typical expectations:
- p50: <1ms
- p95: <5ms
- p99: <10ms (threshold line)

Write latencies may spike during:
- Compaction operations
- Checkpoint/snapshot creation
- High write throughput periods
panels.title: Disk Write Latency

panels.description:
Memory usage breakdown by component:

- RAM Index: In-memory spatial index for fast lookups
- Cache: Block cache for frequently accessed data
- Other: Memtables, buffers, overhead

Understanding memory distribution helps tune cache sizes and identify memory pressure sources.
panels.title: Memory by Component

panels.description:
Total size of the data file(s) on disk. This tracks storage growth over time.

Data file size reflects:
- Active data volume
- Space amplification from LSM structure
- Pending garbage from deletions (reclaimed by compaction)

Sudden drops indicate successful compaction reclaiming space.
panels.title: Data File Size

panels.description:
Time taken to complete checkpoint (snapshot) operations. Checkpoints create consistent recovery points.

Checkpoint duration depends on:
- Data volume to snapshot
- Disk write speed
- Concurrent write load

Long checkpoints may temporarily impact write latency.
panels.title: Checkpoint Duration

panels.description:
Number of checkpoints completed in the last hour.

Checkpoint frequency is configurable. Typical settings:
- Production: Every 5-15 minutes
- High-availability: More frequent (1-5 min)
- Batch workloads: Less frequent (30-60 min)

Zero checkpoints may indicate checkpoint mechanism issues.
panels.title: Checkpoints/hour

list.label: Terminology
list.name: terminology
list.query: archerdb : archerdb, database : database, plain : plain

title: ArcherDB Storage
